name: Snatch

load.raw: Own snatch max; 70–95% by phase (the single reference for all snatch-based work)

contraindications: Acute wrist/shoulder/low-back injury → regress to variations
raw_fault: This IS the sport — not a corrective. Builds full-lift make-rate, speed under the bar and competition specificity; every fault is ultimately rehearsed and expressed here.

name: Clean & Jerk
equipment_sub: Train the Clean and the Jerk separately when fatigue or skill demands
raw_fault: This IS the sport — not a corrective. Builds full-lift make-rate and competition specificity across both the clean and the jerk.

name: Clean
raw_fault: Jerk fatigue caps clean volume when always trained as a full C&J → training the clean alone lets the athlete accumulate quality clean reps and drills recovery from the deep catch without the jerk limiting the dose.

name: Split Jerk (from rack)
equipment_sub: No rack → jerk from the floor (clean it first) or use jerk blocks
cues: drive the bar not the body
punch under into a stable split
raw_fault: Jerk is missed in the C&J because clean fatigue steals jerk practice → jerking fresh from the rack isolates the dip-drive and split so the most-missed phase gets dedicated, unfatigued reps.

name: Power snatch
quality: Speed / rate of force (extension + fast turnover)
intensity: Moderate → Heavy (relative to its own cap)
raw_fault: Low, slow catch with a soft turnover because the athlete pulls high then crashes under → the power snatch forces a higher, faster catch and rewards complete extension, sharpening turnover speed and bar elevation.

name: Hang snatch (above knee / high hang)
raw_fault: Bar swings away / incomplete second pull because the athlete rushes the finish from the floor → starting above the knee removes the first pull and drills a vertical, complete second-pull extension with the bar kept close.

name: Hang snatch (below knee)
raw_fault: Hips shoot up and bar drifts forward because the athlete loses the back angle early → starting below the knee trains holding the shoulders over the bar through the knee and a patient sweep, correcting the early hip rise.

name: Snatch from blocks (above knee)
quality: Pulling strength / second-pull power from a dead stop
equipment_sub: No blocks → hang snatch from the same height
raw_fault: Weak/slow second pull because there is no stretch-reflex help → a dead-stop start above the knee forces the athlete to generate the finish from a static position, building second-pull power and positional strength.

name: Snatch from blocks (below knee)
quality: First-pull start-position strength from a dead stop
equipment_sub: No blocks → hang snatch below knee
raw_fault: Slow off the floor / poor start position because the athlete can't hold the correct back angle under load → a dead-stop below-knee block start overloads the exact start position and builds first-pull strength without accumulating floor-lift fatigue.

name: Deficit snatch
quality: Pulling strength / positional (first pull, posterior, extended ROM)
equipment_sub: No deficit → snatch deadlift with a pause below the knee
raw_fault: Fast/weak off the floor with early hip rise because the posterior chain gives out low → standing on a deficit lengthens the first pull and raises time-under-tension at the bottom, forcing stronger, more patient back-and-leg extension. Excluded from the peak: too fatiguing and it degrades positions when sharpness matters.

name: Pause / segment snatch (pause at knee)
raw_fault: Rushing through the knee and losing position because the athlete is impatient off the floor → a 2–3 s pause at the knee kills momentum and forces the athlete to own the position and finish correctly from it.

name: No-feet snatch
quality: Technique/positional (vertical finish, staying over the bar)
raw_fault: Snatch missed forward because the athlete jumps back and swings the bar away from the body → the no-feet snatch removes the foot re-set, forcing them to keep the bar over the base and finish vertically.

name: Tempo / slow-pull snatch
cues: Slow and correct to the knee
raw_fault: Bar path drifts and positions blur because the athlete moves too fast to self-correct → a controlled tempo first pull ingrains the correct bar path and back angle and makes positional faults visible and fixable.

name: Muscle snatch
cues: Pull all the way to lockout
raw_fault: Slow, late turnover and soft lockout because the athlete relies on dropping under rather than pulling the bar high → the muscle snatch (no re-bend of the legs) trains a continuous pull to lockout, speeding turnover and strengthening the finish.

name: Tall snatch
raw_fault: Slow pull under and late lockout because the athlete waits for the bar → starting tall on flat feet with no leg drive forces an aggressive fast pull under and punch to lockout, training pure speed under the bar.

name: No-hook snatch / clean

load.raw: 60–75% of the matched lift (snatch or clean)

raw_fault: Early arm pull / bar spins away because the athlete yanks with the hands and hook grip masks it → removing the hook forces staying connected and finishing with the legs and hips, exposing and correcting an early arm bend.

name: Overhead squat
quality: Overhead stability + snatch bottom position + mobility
equipment_sub: No rack → snatch/ snatch-balance the bar into position
contraindications: Unstable / injured shoulders → regress or avoid
raw_fault: Unstable or collapsing snatch catch because the athlete lacks strength and mobility in the bottom overhead position → the overhead squat builds active overhead stability and comfort in the deep catch so a received bar is controlled.

name: Snatch balance
quality: Speed under + overhead stability + confidence receiving heavy
raw_fault: Hesitant, high catch because the athlete fears dropping under a heavy bar → the snatch balance drives the athlete fast under a supra-snatch load into a locked bottom, training speed under and confidence receiving heavy.

name: Drop snatch
quality: Speed under / footwork into the catch
raw_fault: Slow feet and a soft catch because the athlete rides the bar down → the drop snatch (no dip-drive) trains punching the feet out and locking the bottom fast with no leg contribution, sharpening the receiving position.

name: Sots press (snatch grip)
quality: Overhead stability + thoracic/shoulder mobility in the bottom
contraindications: Shoulder/thoracic restriction → regress load or skip
raw_fault: Bar drifts forward and torso tips in the catch because the athlete lacks upright overhead position in a full squat → pressing from the bottom of a squat builds the exact mobility and stability to hold the bar overhead with an upright torso.

name: Snatch lift-off (halting DL to knee)
raw_fault: Shoulders roll behind and hips shoot on the first pull because the athlete can't hold the start posture → a slow lift-off to the knee (then reset) overloads holding shoulders-over-bar off the floor, reinforcing the correct first-pull position.

name: Power clean
quality: Speed / rate of force (extension + fast high rack)
raw_fault: Slow turnover and low elbows because the athlete pulls the bar into the shoulders → the power clean rewards full extension and a fast, high rack, sharpening turnover and elbow speed.

name: Hang clean (above knee)
raw_fault: Early arm bend and incomplete extension from the floor → the above-knee start drills a complete vertical second pull with a patient finish, keeping the bar close.

name: Hang clean (below knee)
raw_fault: Hips rise early and the bar loops out because the athlete loses the back angle through the knee → the below-knee start trains holding shoulders over the bar and a patient sweep, fixing early hip rise.

name: Clean from blocks (above knee)
quality: Second-pull power from a dead stop
equipment_sub: No blocks → hang clean at the same height
raw_fault: Weak second pull without stretch-reflex help → a dead-stop block start above the knee forces finishing power from a static position, building second-pull strength.

name: Deficit clean
equipment_sub: No deficit → clean deadlift with a pause below the knee
cues: hold the angle to the knee
raw_fault: Weak, early-rising first pull because the posterior chain fails low → a deficit lengthens the first pull and overloads a patient, posterior-driven start. Kept out of the peak: too fatiguing and position-degrading near a max.

name: Pause / segment clean (pause at knee)
raw_fault: Rushing through the knee and losing position → a 2–3 s pause at the knee removes momentum and forces owning the position before a correct finish.

name: Clean lift-off (halting DL to knee)
raw_fault: Hips shoot and shoulders fall behind off the floor because the athlete can't hold the start posture → a slow halting lift-off to the knee overloads the exact first-pull position and back angle.

name: Jerk from blocks
quality: Overhead + drive from a dead stop (heavy overload)
equipment_sub: No blocks → jerk from the rack
raw_fault: The jerk can't be trained heavy because the athlete is too fatigued from the clean → jerk blocks let the athlete overload the jerk fresh and heavy from a dead stop, building drive and lockout without cleaning the bar.

name: Power jerk (push jerk)
raw_fault: Weak drive and reliance on the split to rescue a soft jerk → the power jerk removes the split, forcing a powerful vertical drive and fast drop into a partial squat, building drive and speed under.

name: Behind-the-neck jerk
quality: Drive path / vertical bar path + overhead
equipment_sub: Front jerk with a strict vertical-drive cue
cues: drive the bar not the head
raw_fault: Bar drives forward and the athlete jerks around the face → jerking from behind the neck grooves a straight vertical drive and overhead position without the bar-around-chin detour.

name: Jerk dip + drive
quality: Leg drive / dip mechanics (partial ROM overload)
raw_fault: Soft, forward, or bent dip because the athlete lacks a controlled vertical dip and explosive drive → the jerk dip+drive overloads a straight-down dip and vertical drive, teaching the legs to launch the bar.

name: Jerk recovery (overhead support / walkout)
raw_fault: Bar crashes or drifts after the split because the athlete can't stabilize a heavy load overhead → holding/recovering a load above jerk max builds the overhead support strength to finish heavy jerks.

name: Jerk balance (split footwork drill)
quality: Split footwork + timing under the bar
raw_fault: Inconsistent or short split with the bar landing forward → the jerk balance drills starting with the front foot forward and punching under a static bar, grooving footwork and a stable overhead landing.

name: Pause jerk (pause in the dip)
raw_fault: Athlete dips crooked or rebounds too fast and loses the drive line → a pause at the bottom of the dip removes the bounce and forces a balanced dip before a vertical drive.

name: Snatch pull
quality: Pulling strength + full extension (raises bar-elevation ceiling)
raw_fault: Bar elevation is capped by pulling strength → the snatch pull overloads the full extension pattern above snatch weight, raising how much bar the athlete can elevate and reinforcing a complete finish.

name: Clean pull
cues: stay over the bar to the hip
raw_fault: Clean bar elevation is capped by pulling strength → the clean pull overloads the extension pattern above clean weight, raising the ceiling and grooving a complete finish.

name: Snatch high pull
quality: Extension + top-of-pull speed (high elbows, upper back)
raw_fault: Incomplete extension and a low, early pull → the high pull demands finishing the extension and pulling the bar high with the elbows up, training the top of the pull and turnover intent.

name: Clean high pull
raw_fault: Cutting the extension and pulling early with the arms → the clean high pull trains a full finish then a high elbow pull, sequencing the top of the pull correctly.

name: Snatch deadlift
raw_fault: Weak or unstable start position and slow floor speed → the snatch-grip deadlift overloads the start posture and posterior chain, strengthening the positions the pull is built on.

name: Clean deadlift
raw_fault: Weak start position and slow first pull in the clean → the clean-grip deadlift overloads the exact start posture and posterior chain.

name: Deficit snatch pull
equipment_sub: No deficit → segment snatch pull with a pause below knee
cues: hold the angle to the knee
raw_fault: Weak, early-rising first pull → a deficit lengthens the first pull and overloads a patient, posterior-driven start. Excluded from the peak: fatiguing and it can degrade positions near a max.

name: Deficit clean pull
equipment_sub: No deficit → segment clean pull with a pause below knee
raw_fault: Weak, early-rising first pull in the clean → a deficit lengthens and overloads a patient posterior-driven start. Excluded from the peak.

name: Segment pull (pause below knee)

load.raw: 90–100% of the matched lift (snatch or clean)

raw_fault: Rushing the first pull and losing the back angle → a pause below the knee ingrains holding position before the finish, correcting an early hip rise or bar drift.

name: Back squat
quality: Max strength — the general engine of the total
contraindications: Knee/low-back flags → box or tempo squat, cut depth/volume
raw_fault: The total is capped by leg/squat strength → the back squat builds the general strength engine underpinning standing out of cleans and driving the jerk. Priority stays high until ratios show strength-sufficiency.

name: Front squat
quality: Leg drive / clean recovery + upright trunk

load.raw: Own front-squat max (or 84–88% of back squat if untested)

contraindications: Knee (deep), wrist/elbow mobility → strap or regress
raw_fault: Stuck in the bottom of the clean / can't recover → the front squat directly overloads the clean-recovery position and upright trunk, raising the weight the athlete can stand up with.

name: Pause back squat
quality: Starting strength + position out of the bottom
raw_fault: Bounces out of the bottom and collapses forward → a 2–3 s pause removes the stretch reflex and builds strength and an upright position from a dead bottom.

name: Pause front squat
quality: Clean-recovery strength from a dead bottom
raw_fault: Buried and stalls at the bottom of a clean → the paused front squat builds the ability to start the drive from a dead stop in the exact recovery position.

name: Tempo squat (back or front)
raw_fault: Loses position and control descending under load → a slow eccentric builds control, position awareness and muscle/tendon capacity, especially in the off-season.

name: Strict press (overhead press)

load.raw: Own strict-press max, or ~60–70% of push press

raw_fault: Weak overhead lockout and the jerk fails overhead → strict pressing builds the raw overhead pressing strength and shoulder base from which the jerk is expressed.

name: Push press
quality: Overhead drive strength (leg drive to lockout)

load.raw: Own push-press max, or ~75–85% of jerk

raw_fault: Weak jerk drive and overhead → the push press overloads the leg-drive-to-overhead pattern heavier than a jerk allows, building drive and lockout strength that feeds the jerk.

name: Behind-the-neck snatch-grip press
quality: Overhead mobility + snatch-lockout strength / upper back
equipment_sub: Sots press (snatch grip) if mobility allows
raw_fault: Weak, unstable overhead in the wide snatch grip and a tight receiving position → snatch-grip BTN pressing builds overhead strength and mobility specific to the snatch lockout.

name: Barbell row (Pendlay / bent-over)

load.raw: RPE 7–8, 6–10 reps (~50–70% of clean as a starting reference)

raw_fault: Weak upper back lets the bar drift and positions collapse under load → heavy rows build the upper-back mass and strength to hold positions in the pull and rack.

name: Weighted pull-up
raw_fault: Weak lats and grip undercut a strong pull and stable overhead → pull-ups build the lat and grip strength that support the pull and overhead positions.

name: Romanian deadlift (RDL)

load.raw: RPE 7–8, 5–8 reps (~50–70% of clean deadlift as a reference)

raw_fault: Weak posterior chain lets the hips shoot and the back angle fail off the floor → RDLs build the hamstring and back mass/strength to hold pull positions. Emphasized in the off-season, trimmed toward the peak.

name: Good morning
contraindications: Acute low-back pain → avoid; keep light
raw_fault: Back rounds and hips rise early as the erectors/hamstrings fatigue → good mornings build the hip-hinge posterior strength to keep the back angle under pulling load.

name: Glute-ham raise (GHR)
quality: Hamstring strength through the knee + joint health
raw_fault: Weak hamstrings limit extension and knee stability → GHRs build hamstring strength through the knee, supporting a strong finish and healthy knees.

name: Back extension (hyperextension)

load.raw: Bodyweight or light held plate; RPE 7; 10–15 reps

raw_fault: Erectors fatigue and the back angle collapses deep into pulling volume → back extensions build the erector endurance to hold position late in sessions.

name: Weighted front plank

load.raw: Bodyweight ± plate on the back; 20–60 s holds

raw_fault: Trunk leaks under the bar in the dip/drive and receiving → anti-extension bracing builds the midline stiffness to transmit force and stay rigid overhead.

name: Hanging leg raise / toes-to-bar
raw_fault: Weak trunk control lets the pelvis and ribcage disorganize under load → hanging leg raises build controlled trunk flexion and bracing without a mobile spine.

name: Loaded carry (front-rack / farmer)
quality: Braced trunk stiffness + grip under time
raw_fault: Trunk and grip give out holding heavy positions → loaded carries build braced trunk stiffness and grip; the front-rack carry directly loads the clean rack position.

name: Leg press
quality: Quad hypertrophy / leg strength (low systemic cost)

load.raw: RPE 7–9, 8–15 reps (own machine load — not a lift %)

equipment_sub: No machine → goblet squat or Bulgarian split squat for volume
raw_fault: Leg/quad size caps the total but heavy squatting is fatiguing → the leg press adds high-volume quad hypertrophy with low skill and spinal cost, ideal in the off-season.

name: Leg extension
equipment_sub: No machine → Spanish squat or sissy squat (banded)
raw_fault: Weak quads/VMO and knees that ache under squat volume → controlled leg extensions build quad mass and, done slow, help patellar-tendon tolerance.

name: Leg curl (seated/lying)
equipment_sub: No machine → Nordic curl or slider leg curl
raw_fault: Hamstrings under-built relative to quads → knee-flexion curls add direct hamstring mass the compound lifts under-target, protecting the knee and balancing the leg.

name: Bulgarian split squat
equipment_sub: No bench → reverse lunge or split squat
raw_fault: Side-to-side leg asymmetry and weak single-leg drive → the Bulgarian split squat builds unilateral leg strength/mass and evens the drive out of the clean.

name: Nordic hamstring curl

load.raw: Bodyweight eccentric, 4–8 reps (add tempo/load as able)

raw_fault: Hamstring strains and weak eccentric control → the Nordic overloads the hamstring eccentric, the strongest evidence-based reducer of hamstring injury.

name: Hip thrust
equipment_sub: No setup → glute bridge or RDL
raw_fault: Weak hip extension/glutes leave the finish of the pull soft → hip thrusts build direct glute and hip-extension strength/mass to power extension.

name: Reverse hyperextension
equipment_sub: No machine → GHD back extension or back extension
contraindications: Acute low-back pain → keep very light/skip
raw_fault: Low back beaten up by pulling volume → the reverse hyper trains the posterior chain while decompressing the spine, a joint-friendly low-back builder.

name: GHD back extension
equipment_sub: No GHD → back-extension bench or good morning
cues: Round then extend to a long spine
raw_fault: Erectors/glutes/hams fatigue and the back angle collapses in the pull → GHD raises build posterior-chain strength-endurance through a full range to hold positions late.

name: Chest-supported row
raw_fault: Weak upper back but a low back already taxed by pulls → chest-supported rows add upper-back mass with the torso braced, sparing the spine.

name: Lat pulldown
equipment_sub: No cable → band pulldown or pull-ups
raw_fault: Lats/upper back need volume weaker athletes can't get from pull-ups → the lat pulldown adds scalable lat hypertrophy supporting the pull and overhead.

name: Barbell shrug
quality: Trap / upper-back strength (finish of the pull)
raw_fault: Weak traps cut the finish of the pull short and struggle to stabilize a heavy bar → heavy shrugs build the traps that complete extension and hold position.

name: Dumbbell overhead press
raw_fault: Overhead pressing volume and shoulder mass build a bigger, healthier jerk lockout → DB pressing adds unilateral overhead hypertrophy and stability.

name: Lateral raise
raw_fault: Underdeveloped delts destabilize the overhead catch → lateral raises build deltoid mass and the stability that supports a locked-out bar.

name: Face pull
quality: Rear delt / external rotators — posture & shoulder health (prehab)
raw_fault: Rounded, forward shoulders from heavy pulling threaten the overhead position → face pulls build rear delts and external rotators, balancing posture and protecting the shoulder overhead.

name: Band external rotation
equipment_sub: Cable → band, or side-lying DB ER
raw_fault: Weak rotator cuff undermines a stable overhead lockout → external rotations strengthen the cuff so the shoulder stays healthy and locked overhead.

name: Overhead triceps extension
quality: Triceps strength for jerk lockout + hypertrophy
raw_fault: Jerk fails at lockout because the triceps give out overhead → overhead extensions build elbow-extension strength that finishes the jerk plus triceps mass off-season.

name: Triceps pushdown
quality: Triceps hypertrophy + lockout endurance (low joint stress)
equipment_sub: No cable → overhead DB extension or bench dip
raw_fault: Triceps hypertrophy and lockout endurance for overhead → pushdowns add high-rep triceps volume supporting the jerk lockout with minimal joint stress.

name: Close-grip bench press
equipment_sub: No bench → weighted dip or DB press
raw_fault: Weak pressing/triceps undercut the jerk lockout → close-grip bench builds horizontal pressing and triceps strength that transfer to a locked overhead.

name: Weighted dip
equipment_sub: No bars → close-grip bench or push-up
contraindications: Shoulder pain at depth → limit ROM
raw_fault: Triceps and pressing strength for a stable lockout → dips build triceps/chest mass and pressing power supporting the overhead.

name: Biceps curl
raw_fault: Elbow flexors get strained in the rack/catch and biceps are under-built → curls build elbow-flexor strength that protects the catch position and add arm mass.

name: Standing calf raise
raw_fault: Weak calves limit the top of extension and ankle stability → calf raises build the plantarflexors that finish the pull and stabilize the jerk drive and receiving.

name: Tibialis raise
quality: Anterior shin / ankle health + knee tracking (prehab)
raw_fault: Poor ankle control and weak shins affect the squat/receiving and knee tracking → tibialis raises build the anterior shin for ankle health and cleaner knee tracking.

name: GHD sit-up
equipment_sub: No GHD → decline or weighted sit-up
raw_fault: Weak trunk flexion limits bracing under the bar → GHD sit-ups build anterior-chain trunk strength through a full range.

name: Pallof press
raw_fault: Trunk rotates and leaks under asymmetric load (split jerk, single-arm carries) → the Pallof press builds anti-rotation stiffness to keep the midline rigid.

name: Box jump
quality: Reactive lower-body power / rate of force development

load.raw: Bodyweight; box height for quality; 3–5 reps

equipment_sub: No box → broad jump or countermovement (vertical) jump
raw_fault: Poor rate of force development and reactive leg power → box jumps train fast, powerful triple extension with a soft landing, sharpening explosiveness. Keep submaximal and low-volume for quality.

name: Depth jump
raw_fault: Slow reactive strength and a weak stretch-shortening cycle in advanced athletes → depth jumps train the fast eccentric-to-concentric switch. Low volume, full recovery, on healthy legs only.
